_comment: Software Valuation & Technical Due-Diligence — Jupiter (native privacy-first Android file manager). Numbers grounded in growth_strategy/data/repo_scan.json.

executive_summary.description: Jupiter is a native, privacy-first Android file manager (single :app module, com.jupiter.filemanager) that aims to be as simple as Files by Google but as powerful as Solid Explorer / X-plore. It pairs everyday local file management with real LAN/remote backends (SMB, SFTP, FTP, WebDAV), an embedded Wi-Fi desktop-transfer server, an AES-GCM encrypted vault with biometric unlock, broad archive support (zip/tar/gz/7z/rar), WorkManager automation, an in-app PDF/media viewer, and an optional on-device Claude (Anthropic) AI assistant gated behind a user-supplied key. The product is explicitly no-ads, no-dark-patterns. It targets power users, creators, office/students, privacy-conscious users, and LAN/NAS users. Stage: feature-complete pre-revenue MVP — no users yet, not monetised.
executive_summary.callout: ⚠ Biggest Uncertainty Driver: Jupiter has zero users and zero revenue — it is a pre-monetisation MVP. With no installs, retention, or ARR signal, market value is anchored almost entirely to replacement/IP value (€140K–€320K). A single proof point — a few thousand retained installs or even €10–30K ARR from a privacy-focused subscription — would shift the central estimate from the IP floor (~€220K) toward the €500K–€900K early-traction band.

product_reconstruction.intro: Reconstructed from direct inspection of the com.jupiter.filemanager source tree, app/build.gradle.kts, and the Gradle version catalog.

rows: Local browsing, copy/move/delete/rename, sort/filter, selection, SAF storage volumes
SMB2/3 (SMBJ), SFTP (JSch), FTP/FTPS (commons-net), WebDAV (OkHttp) with a credential store
Embedded NanoHTTPD server for browser-based file transfer over LAN
EncryptedFile AES-GCM entries + plaintext meta sidecar, biometric/Master-key unlock
Sync + automation repositories driven by WorkManager scheduling
In-app PdfRenderer viewer, image/video thumbnails (Coil), text editor
Anthropic Claude Messages API, gated on a user-supplied key; NoOp fallback when absent
Tagging, multi-workspace, file version history, activity log
Scoped storage, SAF/DocumentFile, Android 11+ permission model
Four protocols (SMB/SFTP/FTP/WebDAV), connection lifecycle, credential security, flaky-network handling
Embedded HTTP server, lifecycle, multipart upload, LAN discovery
AES-GCM via EncryptedFile, MasterKey, biometric prompt, sidecar metadata
Five formats, streaming extraction, large-file memory pressure
Anthropic Messages API, key gating, graceful no-key fallback
Material 3, 27 feature screens, state hoisting, theming
Each backend (SMB/SFTP/FTP/WebDAV) is an integration project; testing requires live servers
Crypto correctness, key management, biometric flows carry zero error tolerance
16 commits in one rapid push reflect heavy AI tooling; traditional teams take 2–3× longer
Unit tests exist for models/utils; backends and UI lean on manual QA
minSdk 26 → SDK 35: storage/permission behaviour differs widely across versions/OEMs

subsections.title: Bottom-Up Estimation
subsections.body: The 33,932 lines of Kotlin across 212 files and ~27 feature packages were decomposed into 12 functional areas. Each area was estimated from code volume, number of integrated protocols/SDKs, UI interaction surface, and security/edge-case burden evidenced in the source.

subsections.title: Three-Point PERT
subsections.body: Optimistic / Most Likely / Pessimistic hours per area, combined as E = (O + 4·M + P) / 6, with SD = (P − O) / 6. Ranges are reported throughout — never single points.

subsections.title: Analogous Estimation
subsections.body: Calibrated against comparable Android file managers (Solid Explorer, X-plore, Cx File Explorer, Material Files, Files by Google) and public engineering norms for multi-protocol Android utilities.

h2_blocks.title: Important Distinctions
bullets: Effort ≠ Duration: ~3,159 person-hours can ship in ~7 months (2–3 FTE) or stretch to 18+ months (1 FTE).
Build Cost ≠ Market Value: Replacement cost is the floor; installs, retention and revenue multiply it.
Code ≠ Product: A shipped app also needs Play Store assets, store optimisation, support and onboarding not visible in the repo.
AI-assisted ≠ Traditional: The 16-commit single-push build reflects AI-accelerated development; a conventional team would take 2–3× longer.

rows: Architecture, Compose, Hilt, storage/SAF, remote backends
Feature screens, archive, preview, automation, vault UI
Material 3 system, iconography, onboarding, empty states

h2_blocks.title: Delivery Team Options
callouts:
Solo / Lean (1–2 people, 9–12 months)
1× Senior Android · 0.5× Designer
Lowest cost; matches a bootstrapped indie founder. Likely how this MVP was built.

Balanced (3 people, 6–8 months)
1× Senior Android · 1× Mid Android · 1× Backend/Network (shared Designer/QA)
Recommended for a funded indie or small studio. Parallel local/remote tracks.

Studio Delivery (5 people, 4–6 months)
2× Senior · 1× Mid · 1× Backend/Network · 1× Designer (shared QA/PM)
Maximum velocity. Suitable for agency delivery with hard deadlines.

h2_blocks.title: Detailed Cost Model — Most Likely (CEE Blended Rates)

rows: Closest power-user peer; cloud + LAN (SMB/FTP/WebDAV), strong UX. The bar Jupiter targets on power.
Dual-pane, deep LAN/cloud, Wi-Fi PC sharing; very feature-dense, dated UI.
Clean UX, network (SMB/FTP/WebDAV) support; large install base.
Modern Material UI, archive + FTP/SFTP/SMB; closest design/architecture analogue.
Massive reach, dead-simple UX, cleanup nudges; no real LAN/remote depth. The bar Jupiter targets on simplicity.
Privacy-positioned (no ads in Plus), network + media tools; closest privacy-stance peer.
Not yet chosen; ad-free stance favours paid/subscription
Real LAN + vault + on-device-keyed AI = credible wedge

market_value_estimate.intro: Five valuation lenses are applied and triangulated. Because Jupiter is pre-revenue with no users, the estimate is anchored to replacement/IP value and converges on the project baseline of €140K–€320K.

subsections.title: Lens 1: Replacement / IP Value
subsections.body: Build cost at CEE blended rates is €110K–€255K (most likely €168K). As a clean, marketed IP asset with real LAN backends and an encrypted vault, a 1.0–1.3× multiplier applies. Result: €140,000 – €320,000 — the floor, with zero revenue.

subsections.title: Lens 2: Comparable-Based Valuation
subsections.body: Comparable Android file managers monetise via one-off €2–4 purchases or ads. Pre-traction, comparables imply little above replacement cost; value only compounds with installs. At 10–50K engaged users an ad-free app could support €350K–€900K; at €100–300K ARR, €800K–€2.4M.

subsections.title: Lens 3: Feature-Depth Premium
subsections.body: Four real LAN protocols, an embedded Wi-Fi transfer server, an AES-GCM vault, five archive formats, and an optional Claude assistant exceed what most free file managers ship. Against the replacement floor this supports a 1.1–1.3× depth premium — already folded into Lens 1's upper bound.

subsections.title: Lens 4: Risk Adjustment
subsections.body: Haircuts: zero users/retention proof (−15%), thin automated test coverage on backends/UI (−10%), single-contributor key-person risk (−10%), unproven monetisation (−10%). Offsetting: modern Kotlin/Compose/Clean stack (+10%), privacy/no-ads positioning (+5%), CI building debug+release APKs (+5%). Net: ≈ −25%, which keeps the central estimate near the IP floor.

subsections.title: Lens 5: Strategic / Acqui-Hire Value
subsections.body: To a privacy-focused app portfolio, an OEM, or a NAS vendor (e.g. Synology/QNAP-adjacent), the LAN stack + vault + clean architecture have acqui-hire value of 1.5–2.2× IP for the team and codebase. Range: €250K – €700K in a strategic deal.

market_value_estimate.callout: Triangulated Current Value: €140K – €320K (central ≈ €220K). As a pre-revenue, zero-user MVP, Jupiter is valued essentially at replacement/IP cost. Demonstrating retained installs or modest ARR is the single highest-leverage way to move into the €350K–€900K early-traction band.

h2_blocks.title: Hard Evidence (Known)
bullets: Full source: 212 Kotlin files, 33,932 lines of Kotlin, 237 files total (repo scan)
Stack confirmed: Kotlin 2.0 / Compose / Material 3 / MVVM + Clean / Hilt / Coroutines; Gradle 8.14.3 + AGP 8.7.3; minSdk 26 / target+compileSdk 35
Real LAN backends in code: SMBJ (SMB), JSch (SFTP), commons-net (FTP), OkHttp (WebDAV); NanoHTTPD Wi-Fi server
EncryptedFile/MasterKey vault, WorkManager automation, PdfRenderer viewer, archive (commons-compress + xz + junrar)
Anthropic Claude assistant gated on a user key with a NoOp fallback; JUnit unit tests; CI builds debug + release APKs
Git: 16 commits, single rapid push; version 0.1.0

h2_blocks.title: Inferred (Not Directly Observed)
bullets: Build effort (estimated from code volume, protocol count, and security complexity)
Rate assumptions (CEE blended €45–€75/h market norms)
Comparable products and their monetisation (public store listings)
AI-acceleration credit on cost (inferred from 16 commits in one push)

h2_blocks.title: Unknown / Missing
bullets: Users / installs / MAU — none; the app is unreleased
Revenue / ARR / monetisation model — not yet chosen; most dominant valuation variable
Retention, crash-free rate, store ratings — no telemetry or Play Console data
Real signing keystore — release currently signed with the debug key (placeholder)

h2_blocks.title: For Valuation Uplift (Sale / Fundraising)
bullets: Ship to Play Store and instrument installs/retention — even 5–20K engaged users changes the valuation band
Pick a monetisation model (one-off unlock or privacy-first subscription) and prove €10–30K early ARR
Publish a landing page and ASO assets emphasising the no-ads / privacy / real-LAN wedge

h2_blocks.title: For Technical Hardening
bullets: Replace the debug signing config with a real release keystore before publishing
Expand automated coverage to the four remote backends and the vault (highest-risk, lowest-tested areas)
Add crash reporting and privacy-respecting analytics to generate the retention evidence valuation needs

h2_blocks.title: For Product Differentiation
bullets: Lean into the Claude assistant as a marketed feature (smart rename, cleanup, search) with clear key-bring-your-own UX
Polish onboarding to hit the 'as simple as Files by Google' bar for non-power users
Document NAS/LAN setup flows to win the LAN/NAS persona against Solid Explorer / X-plore

rows: Full codebase; clear architecture and protocol surface
Blended rate data available; AI-build credit inferred
No users/revenue; anchored to IP floor
Monetisation model unchosen; comparables are one-off/ad-based

appendix.closing: This report was produced by AI-assisted technical due-diligence combining direct repository inspection of the Jupiter Android codebase and external market research on consumer Android file managers. It is for informational purposes and does not constitute financial advice.
